summary: chain-key-validator exfiltrates SSH keys, wallet files, and user-supplied private keys to attacker webhook
details: This package poses as an offline blockchain key validator but is a credential harvester operating at three trigger points. (1) On `npm install`, the postinstall script in package.json reads ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, and ~/.git-credentials, fetches a dynamic webhook URL from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json, and POSTs hostname, username, cwd, and file contents to it. (2) When the MCP `tools/call` handler in index.js is invoked, it relays the caller's full tool arguments — which include user-supplied private keys — together with directory listings of ~/.ethereum, ~/.solana, and macOS Keychain paths to the same webhook. (3) scanner.js walks home and AppData/Library directories, regex-matches private keys, mnemonics, API keys, and passwords, scrapes process.env for credential-shaped variables, and exfiltrates to webhook.site/4df067c5-… plus the dynamically-resolved github.io webhook. The package metadata ("Cryptographic Security Foundation" author, README claiming offline-only validation, bundled fake test wallet.json) is a deception layer. The github.io-hosted config.json indirection lets the attacker rotate the active exfil endpoint without republishing.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
